# L,D,&C Call Prep - Jan 30, 2026

**Time:** 11am EST
**Attendees:** Caroline, Diane, Leslye
**Context:** Contract auto-terminates today. Major quality issues discovered yesterday.

---

## Your Position

**Paid:** $6,700 (100%)
**Received:** Nothing usable

The samples are defective. The patterns created those defective samples - so the patterns are also wrong. You paid in full and have nothing you can take to a factory.

Caroline admitted she wasn't checking the BOM - only callouts and graded specs. But the patterns also don't match specs, which is Diane's responsibility. And Diane confirmed that Leslye didn't actually measure the samples - she assumed specs were correct and copied some of them instead of verifying.

---

## What You Want

**Primary goal:** Partial refund for defective work. You paid $6,700 and received nothing usable.

**Fallback:** Corrected patterns + samples made from them to verify they're actually correct. Patterns alone are just files - you can't know they work until they're sewn.

**The problem:** Even if they "fix" the patterns, you have no way to verify without samples. And you've lost confidence in their ability to execute samples correctly.

---

## Opening Statement

> "I've paid $6,700 in full and I don't have anything I can use. The samples are wrong - wrong fabric, missing construction, specs that don't match what we agreed to. But the samples come from the patterns, which means the patterns are also wrong.
>
> Caroline, you said you weren't checking the BOM. Diane, the patterns don't reflect the email revisions we agreed on. I can't take these patterns to a factory and get correct samples.
>
> The contract ends today. I paid in full for deliverables I can't use. What are we going to do about that?"

---

## Key Points to Make

### 1. The patterns are wrong too

The samples come from the patterns. If the samples don't match spec, the patterns don't match spec. You can't take these patterns to another sample maker and get correct garments - you'd get the same wrong results.

### 2. This isn't the first QC failure

- **Oct 31:** Leslye flagged samples measuring off spec
- **Dec 6:** G-String XS/S didn't fit, specs were wrong
- **Dec 8:** Diane acknowledged they "fell out of normal QC protocols"
- **Jan 29:** Wrong fabric, missing construction, specs ignored

Each time there was an explanation (timeline pressure, factory files, etc.) but the result is the same: I don't have usable samples.

### 3. All three of them failed QC

- **Caroline** wasn't checking the BOM (fabric, construction details) - but has been more accountable/communicative
- **Diane** acknowledged QC breakdown, patterns don't match email revisions
- **Leslye** didn't measure the samples - Diane confirmed she "copied some" specs instead of verifying

This wasn't one person's mistake. The whole QC process broke down.

**Note:** If you continue with the Cotton Mid-Rise Thong, you'd be working primarily with Caroline (sample maker). Diane (patterns) and Leslye (tech packs) have already delivered their work for that style.

### 4. "Corrected patterns" isn't a solution

Patterns are just files. You can't verify they're correct until you make samples from them. If they offer to fix the patterns, you still need samples made to confirm the pieces fit together and hit spec. Corrected patterns alone don't give you anything usable.

---

## What They Might Say & How to Respond

### "This is normal for sample development"

> "I understand samples go through iterations. But iterations are for fit and minor adjustments - not for using the wrong fabric or missing construction methods that are clearly documented. These aren't refinements, they're fundamental errors."

### "We can fix the patterns"

> "Patterns are just files. I can't verify they're correct until samples are made from them. Are you offering to make corrected samples too? Because corrected patterns alone don't give me anything I can use - I'd still need to pay someone else to make samples to find out if they actually work."

### "We can fix it if you extend the contract"

> "The contract has been extended once already when the scope changed. We've been through multiple rounds of revisions. All three of you had a role in QC and all three failed - Caroline wasn't checking the BOM, Diane acknowledged the QC breakdown, and Leslye didn't actually measure the samples. At this point I've lost confidence in the process."

### "We've put in a lot of work"

> "I'm sure you have. But I paid $6,700 for deliverables, not effort. I don't have usable patterns. I don't have usable samples. I have to start over with someone else."

---

## Financial Breakdown

| Deliverable | Contract Value | Status | Usable? |
|-------------|---------------|--------|---------|
| Tech Packs (5) | $1,500 | Delivered | Maybe (reference only) |
| Patterns (5 styles) | $1,800 | Delivered Jan 22 | **No** - don't match specs |
| Samples (17) | $3,400 | Partial | **No** - defective |
| **Total** | **$6,700** | | |

**You've paid:** $6,700 (100%)
**Usable value received:** Near zero
**Loss:** ~$6,700 (minus whatever value the tech packs have as reference docs)

---

## Possible Outcomes

### Best case
They acknowledge the failures and offer a partial refund. Even $2,000-3,000 back would offset having to redo the work elsewhere.

### Middle case
They offer to correct the patterns AND make corrected samples to prove they work. This requires extending the relationship, but at least you'd walk away with verified deliverables.

### Likely case
They offer to "fix the patterns" only. Problem: patterns are just files. You can't verify they're correct without samples. You'd be taking their word for it after they've already failed multiple times.

### Worst case
They defend the work and offer nothing. You walk away with $6,700 loss and have to start from scratch. Consider small claims court if you want to pursue it (you have documented admissions of fault from all three of them).

---

## Questions to Ask

1. "Caroline, can you walk me through what happened with the High Waist Hipster fabric?"

2. "Diane, the samples don't match the specs we agreed to in our emails. The patterns created those samples. How did that happen?"

3. "I paid $6,700 and I don't have patterns I can take to a factory to get correct samples. What are you prepared to do about that?"

4. "If we're talking about correcting the patterns - what's the timeline, and how do I know they'll be right this time?"

---

## Documents to Have Ready

- [ ] Tech pack links (all 5)
- [ ] Sample tracker showing cancelled/paused items
- [ ] Dec 8 email where Diane acknowledged QC breakdown
- [ ] Text messages showing spec revision agreements
- [ ] Caroline's message admitting she wasn't checking BOM
- [ ] Jan 14 text where Diane confirms Leslye didn't measure samples ("she said other than the sticky notes everything was good as is so she did copy some of them")

---

## After the Call

1. **Document the outcome** - Whatever you agree to, follow up with an email summarizing: "Per our call, we agreed that [X]. Please confirm."

2. **If they offer to correct patterns only** - Push back. Patterns alone can't be verified. You need samples made from them to confirm they work. Otherwise you're taking their word for it after they've already failed.

3. **If they offer patterns + samples** - Get a timeline, specify exactly what needs to match (email revisions + tech pack specs), and get confirmation they'll use the correct fabric/construction this time.

4. **If they offer nothing** - Send a formal letter requesting partial refund, citing the documented admissions from all three (Caroline: BOM, Diane: QC breakdown, Leslye: didn't measure samples). This creates a paper trail for small claims if needed.

---

---

## The Photoshoot Problem

**Photoshoot date: February 10th** (11 days from today)

**What you need:**
- Cotton Mid-Rise Thong in grey + teal (samples not made yet)
- G-String (current ones have waistbands too small - dig into model, can't use on-body)
- High Waist Hipster in grey + teal (current ones wrong fabric, wrong fit, can't use)

**The constraints:**
- Only 1 yard grey fabric, 1 yard teal fabric - that's ALL you have
- Can't get more - supplier is in China, closed for Chinese New Year
- Cotton Mid-Rise Thong samples haven't been made yet
- Finding a new sample maker has same QC risk (unfamiliar with their work)

**What you can still use:**
- Pink G-String for flat lay (but you already had old production samples that could do this)
- If Cotton Mid-Rise Thong is slightly off, could still use for flat lay shots (not on-model)

**Caroline's Jan 28 estimate:** Grey/teal hipsters could ship 2/5 or 2/6 IF thread arrives and IF black samples fit. But now we know the black samples have fit issues beyond just the seam.

### The Decision Tree

**For High Waist Hipster:** Probably dead for the photoshoot. Fit is wrong, not just fabric. Would need multiple rounds to fix. You don't have time or extra fabric.

**For G-String:** Current samples can't be used on-model (waistband too small). Could potentially use for flat lay, but you already have old samples that work for that.

**For Cotton Mid-Rise Thong:** This is the only style where you might get something new and usable for the shoot.

### Options for Cotton Mid-Rise Thong

**Option A: Walk away completely**

Don't make any more samples. Find a new sample maker after the shoot.

- **Pro:** No more risk with this team
- **Con:** No new samples for photoshoot. $6,700 loss.

**Option B: Test sample in scrap fabric first**

Caroline makes Cotton Mid-Rise Thong in cheap/different fabric. You verify it hits spec. If it passes, she makes grey and teal.

- **Pro:** Protects your limited fabric
- **Pro:** Proves she can execute before using precious materials
- **Con:** Adds time - may not make Feb 10 deadline
- **Con:** Still working with people who've had QC issues

**Option C: Go straight to grey and teal**

Skip the test, have Caroline make the final samples directly.

- **Pro:** Fastest path - might make the photoshoot
- **Con:** If wrong, you've wasted your only fabric and have nothing

**Option D: Make ONE test in grey or teal, hold the other**

Make one size in one color. You verify. If correct, make the rest.

- **Pro:** Partial protection - only risk half your fabric
- **Pro:** Faster than full test sample in different fabric
- **Con:** Still risking some precious fabric

### Timeline Reality Check

Today: Jan 30 (call)
Thread arrival: Should have arrived Jan 31 (per your email)
Caroline's estimate: Ship 2/5 or 2/6
Photoshoot: Feb 10

That's ~5-6 days for Caroline to cut, sew, and ship. Then 3-4 days for delivery + buffer.

**If you do a test sample first:** Add another 3-4 days for shipping to you, you verify, then ship approval back. That pushes final samples past Feb 10.

**Reality:** If you want Cotton Mid-Rise Thong for the photoshoot, you probably have to skip the test sample and trust Caroline to get it right.

### QC Checkpoints (if you proceed)

Since you can't do a full test sample in time:

1. **Before cutting:** Caroline confirms she's read the BOM and knows the correct fabric + construction
2. **Before sewing:** Caroline measures the cut pattern pieces against the graded specs
3. **After sewing, before shipping:** Caroline measures every POM and sends you photos + measurements for approval before shipping
4. **You verify on receipt:** If wrong, at least you know before the shoot and can pivot to flat lay only

**What to say on the call:**

> "I have a photoshoot on February 10th. The High Waist Hipster isn't going to work - the fit is wrong and there's not enough time to fix it. But I still need the Cotton Mid-Rise Thong in grey and teal.
>
> Here's the problem: I only have 1 yard of grey and 1 yard of teal. That's it. My supplier is closed for Chinese New Year. If you make these wrong, I have nothing.
>
> If we're going to do this, I need you to measure the pattern pieces before you sew, and measure the finished samples before you ship. Send me photos and measurements. I need to approve before you ship. Can you commit to that?"

### The Honest Assessment

You're choosing between:
- **Risk nothing:** Don't make the thongs, use old samples/flat lays for the shoot, find a new team after
- **Risk everything:** Trust Caroline to get it right with QC checkpoints, potentially have new samples for the shoot

Neither option is great. But if the Cotton Mid-Rise Thong is slightly off, you said you could still use it for flat lay. So the worst case isn't total loss - it's just not having on-model shots of that style.

---

---

## CALL SCRIPT

### Part 1: Open with the situation (2 min)

> "Thanks for making time for this call. I want to be direct about where we are, and I'm going to ask that we keep this conversation focused on solutions rather than explanations. I know there were timeline pressures and challenges with the factory files - I've heard those. What I need from this call is to figure out what we're doing next, not rehash what went wrong.
>
> So here's the situation: I've paid $6,700 in full. The contract ends today. The samples don't match specs, the patterns created those samples so they don't match either, and I can't take what I have to a factory and get correct products.
>
> I want to talk about two things: first, moving forward with the Cotton Mid-Rise Thong for my photoshoot. Second, what happens with everything else."

---

### Part 2: The Cotton Mid-Rise Thong (5-10 min)

> "I have a photoshoot on February 10th. The High Waist Hipster isn't going to make it - the fit needs multiple rounds to fix and I don't have time. But I still want to move forward with the Cotton Mid-Rise Thong in grey and teal.
>
> Here's the situation: I only have 1 yard of grey and 1 yard of teal. That's all the fabric I have. My supplier is in China and they're closed for Chinese New Year. If these come out wrong, I can't remake them.
>
> Caroline, I want you to make them. But I need checkpoints this time."

**Then lay out the QC requirements:**

> "Before you cut - confirm you've read the BOM and you have the right fabric and construction details.
>
> Before you ship - measure every POM on the finished samples. Send me photos and the measurements. I need to approve before you put them in the mail.
>
> Can you commit to that?"

**If she agrees:**

> "Great. What's your timeline? The thread should arrive tomorrow. When can you have them done and shipped?"

**If she pushes back on the checkpoints:**

> "I understand it's extra steps. But we've had multiple rounds where samples came back wrong. I can't afford that with this fabric. If the checkpoints don't work for you, then I can't move forward."

**Confirm the details:**

- Thread arriving: Jan 31
- Colors: Grey and Teal
- Sizes: [confirm which sizes]
- Ship by: 2/5 or 2/6 (per her Jan 28 email)
- You approve measurements before shipping

---

### Part 3: The accountability conversation (10-15 min)

> "Now for the harder conversation. I paid $6,700. The High Waist Hipster samples are unusable - wrong fabric, wrong fit. The G-Strings have waistbands that don't work. The High Cut Brief didn't get the revisions. The patterns created those samples, so I can't just take the patterns somewhere else and expect different results.
>
> I'm not asking you to redo everything. But I need to understand what you're prepared to do to make this right."

**Then wait. Let them respond.**

**If they offer to fix patterns:**

> "Patterns are just files. I can't verify they're correct without making samples. And I don't have confidence that samples will come out right based on what's happened. What else can you offer?"

**If they offer to remake samples:**

> "Which samples, in what timeline? I don't have extra fabric for the High Waist Hipster in teal and grey. And the contract ends today."

**If they start explaining/making excuses:**

> "I appreciate you wanting to explain, but I'd like to keep us focused on solutions. What I need to know is what you're able to offer to make this right."

**If they say "this is normal for sample development":**

> "I hear you. But I need to focus on where we go from here, not whether this is typical. What can you offer?"

**If they say "we've put in a lot of work":**

> "I understand. But I paid for deliverables. What I need to know is what you're prepared to do now."

**If they offer nothing:**

> "I want to be clear about what I'm hearing. I paid $6,700, the deliverables don't match specs, you've acknowledged the QC failures, and you're not offering any remedy. Is that correct?"

(This creates a clear statement you can reference later if needed for small claims.)

---

### Part 4: Wrap up and next steps (5 min)

**For the Cotton Thong:**

> "So to confirm - Caroline, you're making the Cotton Mid-Rise Thong in grey and teal. You'll measure before shipping and send me photos and specs for approval. Target ship date is [date]. Correct?"

**For accountability:**

> "And regarding the other styles - [summarize whatever they offered or didn't offer]. I'll follow up with an email summarizing what we discussed."

**Close:**

> "Thank you for your time. I'll send that summary email today."

---

### After the call

**Immediately send an email:**

> Subject: Summary of Jan 30 call
>
> Hi all,
>
> Thank you for the call today. To summarize what we discussed:
>
> **Cotton Mid-Rise Thong:**
> - Caroline will make grey and teal samples
> - Before shipping, Caroline will measure all POMs and send photos + measurements for my approval
> - Target ship date: [date]
>
> **Other styles:**
> - [Summarize whatever was agreed or not agreed]
>
> Please confirm this is accurate.
>
> Best,
> Gabriella

This creates a paper trail regardless of outcome.

---

## Tone

Stay calm and direct. You're not angry - you're stating facts:
- You paid $6,700
- The deliverables don't match specs
- All three admitted fault (Caroline: BOM, Diane: QC breakdown, Leslye: didn't measure)
- You have nothing usable
- Patterns can't be verified without samples

You're not asking for a favor. You paid for work that wasn't delivered correctly. The question is how they're going to make it right.

**On the Cotton Thong:** Be collaborative. Caroline seems willing to make it right. Give her the chance with clear expectations.

**On accountability:** Be firm but not hostile. State facts, let them respond, document everything.
